eli3: Alternative data in finance refers to any data used for investment decisions that is not traditional financial data (prices, earnings, balance sheets). Examples: satellite imagery of retailer parking lots to estimate store traffic before earnings, credit card transaction data to predict quarterly revenue, social media sentiment to gauge investor attention, job postings to measure company growth plans.

The rationale: alternative data can provide an information edge before it is reflected in prices — before the official earnings announcement, an investor with credit card data already knows how well the company's sales are going. If the alternative data is predictive and not yet widely used, it creates a short-lived but genuine alpha source.

Alternative data is now a multi-billion dollar industry. Major hedge funds (Two Sigma, Citadel, D.E. Shaw, Point72) spend hundreds of millions annually on alternative data. The signal half-life is typically short (weeks to months) because adoption spreads and alpha gets arbed away. The most valuable data is hard to acquire, expensive, or requires significant processing.

whenWhy.title: Credit card and transaction data
whenWhy.body: Credit/debit card transaction data: aggregated (anonymised) spending by merchant category. Providers: Second Measure, Earnest Research, Bloomberg Second Measure. Signal construction for retail company ABC: daily/weekly/monthly transaction volume (normalised by historical average) as a real-time sales proxy. Predictive power: credit card data for a major US retailer predicted same-store-sales surprises with IC ≈ 0.3-0.5 (high by equity standards) in early adopter years (2010-2015). As the data became widely used (2018+), the IC decayed to ≈ 0.1-0.2 as the information became priced in faster. Data limitations: (1) Coverage: only a subset of payment networks; not all transactions visible; (2) Geographic bias: US-centric; international coverage is sparser; (3) Privacy regulation: GDPR in Europe limits data availability; (4) Point-in-time: need to reconstruct what data was available at each historical date to build a valid backtest.

whenWhy.title: NLP on earnings calls and SEC filings
whenWhy.body: NLP signals from textual corporate disclosures: (1) Earnings call tone: sentiment analysis (positive/negative word ratio) of management's Q&A responses; managers who hedge more or sound more uncertain → downward revision signal; (2) MD&A (Management Discussion & Analysis) section: year-over-year change in language; new risk factors mentioned → negative signal; (3) SEC 8-K filings: surprise disclosures (management changes, litigation, guidance) → immediate price impact; (4) Analyst report sentiment: aggregated tone of analyst reports → consensus view direction. Tools: FinBERT (BERT fine-tuned on financial text), Loughran-McDonald financial sentiment lexicon. IC of NLP signals: ≈ 0.02-0.05 for earnings call tone; modest individually but additive to price-based signals. Key challenge: earnings call text is available immediately after the call — but the call occurs after market hours; the signal is only usable the next trading day.

whenWhy.title: Satellite and geospatial data
whenWhy.body: Satellite data applications: (1) Parking lot occupancy: count cars at retail store locations via satellite imagery → proxy for foot traffic → predict same-store sales; Spaceknow, RS Metrics provide processed data; (2) Oil storage tank levels: measure the volume of oil in floating-roof storage tanks (roof position indicates fill level); predicts oil inventory reports → crude oil price signal; (3) Shipping traffic: AIS (Automatic Identification System) data from ships → container shipping volumes → global trade activity; (4) Agricultural: crop field health (NDVI index from multispectral imagery) → crop yield predictions → grain price signals; (5) Construction activity: measure change in building footprint or crane count in key cities → GDP/construction sector signal. Processing pipeline: raw satellite imagery (petabytes) → computer vision (object detection, change detection) → normalised signal → merge with financial data → backtest. Main barrier: data is expensive ($100K-$1M/year for quality datasets), requires specialised computer vision processing, and signal half-life is short as adoption grows.

deepDiveIntro: Alternative data ecosystem: (1) Data providers: Eagle Alpha, Quandl (Nasdaq), Bloomberg, Refinitiv specialise in aggregating and distributing alternative data; (2) Procurement process: sign NDA, receive sample data, evaluate signal quality, negotiate license; typical cost: $50K-$500K/year per dataset; (3) Due diligence: assess data quality (coverage, accuracy, historical depth), privacy compliance (GDPR, CCPA), legal review (is using this data permissible? material non-public information risk?), and exclusivity (widely distributed data has less edge); (4) Infrastructure: alternative data requires significant engineering — ingesting, cleaning, transforming, and aligning with financial data in a point-in-time way. Many hedge funds have teams of 10-50 engineers just for alternative data infrastructure.

deepDive.heading: Material Non-Public Information (MNPI) Risks
deepDive.body: The most critical legal risk in alternative data: is the data considered material non-public information (MNPI), and would trading on it constitute insider trading? Legal framework: trading on MNPI is illegal under securities law if obtained by breach of a fiduciary duty. Alternative data grey areas: (1) Expert networks: hedge funds hiring ex-company employees for information — SEC has prosecuted when information was MNPI; (2) Scraped web data: generally legal if publicly available; but if the data was obtained from a company's internal systems (even inadvertently accessible), there's legal risk; (3) Credit card data: aggregated, anonymised transaction data is generally legal (the individual transactions are private but the aggregate is not MNPI); (4) Satellite data: publicly visible from space — legal; (5) App data: user engagement metrics from third-party data providers — legal if properly anonymised. Best practice: legal review of every new data source before use; mosaic theory defence: combining multiple legal public data sources is legal even if the combination provides insight. [1]

deepDive.heading: Signal Decay in Alternative Data
deepDive.body: Alternative data signals decay as more participants obtain and use the same data. Decay pattern: (1) Early adopter advantage (2-5 years post-data availability): high IC (0.3-0.5), large alpha; (2) Mid-adoption (5-10 years): IC decays to 0.1-0.2 as data is incorporated faster; (3) Commoditised (10+ years): IC near 0; alpha fully arbed away. Example: credit card data for US retail was highly alpha-generative for first-movers in 2012-2016; by 2020, almost all major quant funds had the data and signals were weak. The data arms race: firms continuously search for new alternative data sources before they become commoditised. The value is in being first, not in being smarter (after widespread adoption). Mitigations: (1) Proprietary data collection: some firms collect their own data (Citadel owns a pharmacy chain for prescription data; Point72 has proprietary web scraping); (2) Better signal construction: even with the same data, superior processing (ML models, combining with other signals) can extract incremental alpha. [2]

deepDive.heading: Alternative Data Infrastructure and Point-in-Time Construction
deepDive.body: Infrastructure requirements for alternative data: (1) Ingestion pipeline: receive data (API, SFTP, S3 bucket) → validate (schema, coverage checks) → store in data lake (S3/GCS) with timestamps; (2) Point-in-time database: each data record stored with as_of_date (when the fact was known) not just period_date (what period it refers to); essential for valid backtesting; (3) Entity resolution: map company names / tax IDs in alternative data to PERMNO (CRSP) or ISIN (international) — company names change, merge, split; (4) Coverage monitoring: track data coverage over time; gaps in historical data require treatment (interpolation or period exclusion); (5) Signal computation: run signal computation on the PIT database using only data available as of each date; (6) Research platform: Jupyter notebooks with pre-built connectors to the data lake, factor computation library, and walk-forward backtester. Major providers offering integrated alt data + backtesting: Snowflake + Kensho, Bloomberg AIM, proprietary internal systems at large hedge funds. [1]

takeaways: Alternative data: credit cards, satellite, NLP/text, web scraping, geospatial — all sources of pre-earnings information edge. [1]
MNPI risk: aggregated/anonymised data is generally legal; raw company-specific data requires legal review. [1]
Signal decay: early adopter IC 0.3-0.5; commoditised after 5-10 years to near 0. Being first matters most. [2]
PIT infrastructure: every record needs as_of_date not just period_date; mandatory for valid backtesting. [1]
Satellite → parking lots, oil tanks, ships; NLP → earnings call tone, MD&A change, analyst sentiment. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you evaluate the quality of a new alternative dataset before paying for it?
edgeCases.a: Alternative data due diligence process: (1) Request a sample: 3-6 months of data on a subset of companies for free; validate the data structure, coverage (what % of target companies covered), and timeliness (how quickly is data available after the event?); (2) Historical depth: need at least 5-7 years of historical data for meaningful backtesting; new datasets with less history have high backfill risk; (3) Coverage bias: if data covers only large-cap companies (top 500 by revenue), signal may not work for small/mid-cap stocks you want to trade; (4) Backtest the sample: compute the signal on the sample, run a simplified backtest, check IC and factor performance; (5) Legal review: check whether any collection methods could constitute MNPI; review terms of service for scraping restrictions; (6) Sustainability: can the provider maintain data quality at scale? How was the data collected — is the collection method scalable and sustainable? (7) Market adoption: is this data available to many other funds? If so, the alpha half-life is short; negotiate exclusivity or early access windows.

edgeCases.q: What is the role of natural language processing versus structured data in alternative data alpha generation?
edgeCases.a: Both NLP/unstructured data and structured alternative data have distinct properties: NLP (earnings calls, news, social media): (1) High volume of inputs (thousands of texts daily); (2) IC typically low (0.02-0.05); (3) Most valuable for detecting sentiment shifts and unusual language patterns; (4) Short-horizon alpha (days to weeks); (5) Requires domain-specific language models (FinBERT outperforms general BERT on financial text). Structured data (credit cards, satellite counts, app data): (1) Clear numerical signal; (2) IC higher (0.1-0.3) in early-adoption phase; (3) Best for predicting specific fundamental metrics (revenue, units sold); (4) Medium-horizon alpha (weeks to earnings announcements). Combining both: the most predictive models combine NLP signals (management tone, earnings language) with structured signals (transaction data) — each captures different dimensions of the information environment. When both signals align (positive transaction data + confident earnings call tone), signal accuracy improves.

sources.label: Kolanovic, M. & Krishnamachari, R. (2017) — Big Data and AI Strategies, J.P. Morgan
Lopez de Prado, M. — Machine Learning for Asset Managers, Cambridge University Press
